title: Natural statistics support a rational account of confidence biases

frameworks.name: deep neural network model of confidence
frameworks.one_line: DNN computes decision confidence directly from high-dimensional naturalistic stimuli.

frameworks.name: confidence as probability-of-correct prediction
frameworks.one_line: Confidence framed as predicted probability that the decision is correct.

frameworks.name: rational/optimal observer account
frameworks.one_line: Biases arise from optimization to natural sensory input statistics.

frameworks.name: common decision variable hypothesis
frameworks.one_line: Decisions and confidence share a single underlying decision variable.

core_question: Can a model that operates directly on naturalistic stimuli explain puzzling dissociations between decisions and confidence as rational consequences of natural input statistics?
key_assumptions: confidence is a prediction of decision correctness
perceptual systems are optimized for the statistics of natural sensory inputs
high-dimensional naturalistic stimuli are the appropriate domain over which to define confidence
manipulations: stimulus conditions known to produce decision-confidence dissociations
training/operating regime over naturalistic vs idealized inputs
key_findings: DNN-based confidence model reproduces several known decision-confidence dissociations
dissociations emerge naturally from optimization to natural-stimulus statistics
decisions and confidence in the model depend on a common decision variable despite dissociating
interpretations: apparent confidence biases can be rational given natural input statistics
shared decision variable is compatible with observed dissociations between decisions and confidence
